content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


January 20, 2005


Karin Murabito, Deputy City Attorney
City of San Jose
Office of the City Attorney
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, CA 95110

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-245

Dear Ms. Murabito:

This letter is in response to your request, on behalf of the City of San Jose and the City's Finance Director Scott Johnson, for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Would either of the Blue Heron employees you describe be classified as a "consultant" under the Act, and obligated as such to file a Form 700?

2. If the answer to the first question is "yes," would ownership of SPL WorldGroup ("SPL") stock create a conflict of interest under the Act in decisions involving SPL and, if so, would the conflict of interest be removed by sale of the stock?

3. If the answer to the first question is "yes," would the income earned by the spouse of one of these "consultants" create a conflict of interest under the Act in decisions involving SPL?


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

Question 1: Yes, both Blue Heron employees would meet the Act's definition of
"consultant" under the facts presented, and would therefore be "public officials" subject
to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.

Questions 2 and 3: As long as the consultants retain economic interests in SPL, they may
have conflicts of interest in particular governmental decisions, although without more
specific information on upcoming decisions, we can only offer general guidance on
identifying potential conflicts of interest. The consultant whose economic interest in SPL
is grounded in stock ownership would no longer have an economic interest in SPL once
he or she sold that stock. An economic interest in SPL based on income to a consultant's
spouse will come to an end with the passage of time. Specifically, the consultant would
no longer have an economic interest in SPL as a source of income once the consultant's
community property share of that income falls below $500, which would occur when the
consultant's spouse has received or earned less than $1,000 in income from SPL during
the preceding 12 months.

FACTS

In June 2004, the City of San Jose selected BearingPoint/People Soft as its vendor
for the planned purchase, installation, and integration of a computerized utility billing
system, referred to as the CUSP or the CUSP Project.

The CUSP Project is divided into two stages. During stage 1, which may run for
seven months, BearingPoint will provide the city with a detailed analysis of how the
city's existing system will interface with the new PeopleSoft system. During stage 2, an
implementation phase expected to take twelve months, BearingPoint will install the
PeopleSoft system.

City staff will recommend to the city council that it enter into an agreement with a
business entity (Blue Heron), as a third party expert, to work on various tasks during
stage 1 of the CUSP Project. Blue Heron's tasks will include assisting city staff with the
development of the scope of work to be performed by BearingPoint during stage 2, and
assisting city staff with contract negotiations under the direction of the executive steering
committee as discussed below.

As a matter of convenience for the city, Blue Heron's agreement expires on June
30, 2005. However, it is the city's expectation that Blue Heron's services for the initial
phase will be completed no later than March 2005.

While Blue Heron will collaborate with lower level city staff on the development
of the scope of work for BearingPoint, the city's executive steering committee for the
CUSP Project, comprised of the directors of finance, environmental services, the acting
chief information officer, and a deputy city manager, will review and approve all Blue
Heron deliverables, including BearingPoint's scope of work for stage 2, before they are
finalized for purposes of the negotiation. The role of Blue Heron during the contract

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 3

negotiation process will be determined solely by the executive steering committee which
will approve all negotiation strategies and oversee the negotiation process. Assuming that
an agreement is reached with BearingPoint, the contract will be presented to the city
council for its consideration.

You anticipate that staff will recommend to the city council that it enter into a
second agreement with Blue Heron in 2005 for a quality assurance analysis of
BearingPoint's performance during stage 2 at the same time staff brings forward for
approval BearingPoint's contract for stage 2. The anticipated term of the second
agreement with Blue Heron is twelve months, and the estimated compensation is $20,000
per month. Other than the CUSP Project, it does not appear that Blue Heron is under
contract for any other city project.

It is your understanding that BearingPoint will subcontract a portion of the work
to be performed under the agreements to SPL. The city, however, will not have a direct
contractual relationship with SPL. It is your understanding from BearingPoint that the
estimated value of the portion of the stage 1 agreement to be performed by SPL is
$80,000, and the estimated value of the stage 2 agreement to be performed by SPL is
$900,000. It is your understanding that SPL is a privately held company and is not listed
on the New York Stock Exchange, AMEX or NASDAQ.

As discussed above, Blue Heron will assist the city with developing
BearingPoint's scope of work for stage 2 of the CUSP Project. BearingPoint in turn will
subcontract a portion of this work to SPL. It is your understanding that one Blue Heron
employee presently owns stock in SPL valued at more than $15,000. This employee will
act as the lead negotiator in development of BearingPoint's Scope of Work, and will
serve formally or informally as the project manager. It is also your understanding that the
spouse of a second Blue Heron employee was employed by SPL in the Philippines from
May 2002 through January 2004. It is your understanding that the spouse of this second
Blue Heron Employee has, in the twelve months following November 2003, earned
approximately $1,600 from her employment with SPL. This employee will report to the
previously described "project manager," and may perform any of the tasks required of
Blue Heron under its agreement with the city.

"Consultants" are designated employees under the city's conflict of interest code.
Director of Finance Scott Johnson is the official who would be responsible for classifying
individuals working on the CUSP Project as consultants.

ANALYSIS

Question One: Are the Blue Heron Employees "Consultants"?

Before considering any possible conflicts of interest under the Act, it is first
necessary to decide whether the Blue Heron employees are subject to the Act's conflict of
interest rules, which apply only to "public officials" as defined at section 82048.

The Act defines "public official" to include "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." (Section 82048, emphasis added.) The

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 4

San Jose City Council is by definition a "local government agency" under section 82041.
Regulation 18701(a)(2) provides the definition of "consultant" as used in section 82048,
which provides as follows:

"(2) 'Consultant' means an individual who, pursuant to a
contract with a state or local government agency:

(A) Makes a governmental decision whether to:

1. Approve a rate, rule, or regulation;

2. Adopt or enforce a law;

3. Issue, deny, suspend, or revoke any permit, license,
application, certificate, approval, order, or similar
authorization or entitlement;

4. Authorize the agency to enter into, modify, or renew a
contract provided it is the type of contract that requires
agency approval;

5. Grant agency approval to a contract that requires agency
approval and to which the agency is a party, or to the
specifications for such a contract;

6. Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report, study, or
similar item;

7. Adopt, or grant agency approval of, policies, standards, or
guidelines for the agency, or for any subdivision thereof; or

(B) Serves in a staff capacity with the agency and in that
capacity participates in making a governmental decision as
defined in Regulation 18702.2 or performs the same or
substantially all the same duties for the agency that would
otherwise be performed by an individual holding a position
specified in the agency's Conflict of Interest Code under
Government Code Section 87302."

Under the terms of this regulation, a business entity cannot be a "consultant,"
since the term is applied only to an "individual," that is, to a natural person. Thus only
natural persons actually performing duties under the contract may be "consultants."
(Sanchez Advice Letter, No. I-03-173.) Employees of a business who do not perform
work under the contract, or whose duties do not meet the definition of "consultant," are
not "consultants" under the Act. (Del Guercio Advice Letter, No. I-01-116.) Our first
order of business, therefore, is to identify the individuals performing duties under the
contract, and then to determine whether those duties are such that the responsible

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 5

individuals meet the definition of "consultant." In your inquiry you identify two such
persons, and we will confine our discussion to those two Blue Heron employees.

Regulation 18701(a)(2) establishes two standards for qualification as a consultant.
An individual who satisfies either standard is a "consultant" as defined by the Act. First,
an individual may be a "consultant" if he or she performs, pursuant to contract, any of the
actions described in subdivisions (a)(2)(A)(1)-(7) of regulation 18701. Alternatively, an
individual may be a consultant if he or she "serves in staff capacity with the agency"
under subdivision (a)(2)(B).

As we understand the facts, neither of the two identified Blue Heron employees is
authorized to make final, binding decisions on behalf of the city, and in that regard neither
would qualify as a "consultant" under subdivisions (a)(2)(A)(1)-(7) of regulation 18701.
It appears that both of them, however, would be authorized and expected under two
successive "consultancy" contracts to provide ongoing technical recommendations and
advice to the city on the development, management, and execution of the BearingPoint
agreement through stages involving purchase, installation and integration of the CUSP
system. You indicate that the stage 1 contract will last approximately seven months, and
that the stage 2 contract will have a term of twelve months.

From the limited information available to us, we conclude that both of these Blue
Heron employees would be "consultants" to the city council. First, it appears from your
account of the facts they will at least occasionally be called on to "participate in making a
governmental decision" regarding the city's dealings with BearingPoint. Regulation
18702.(2)(b) specifies a person "participates in making a governmental decision" when
he or she:

"(b) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by:
(1) Conducting research or making any investigation
which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the
official and the purpose of which is to influence a
governmental decision referenced in Title 2, California
Code of Regulations, section 18701(a)(2)(A); or
(2) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion,
orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of
which is to influence a governmental decision referenced in
Title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
18701(a)(2)(A)."

It also appears that both of these Blue Heron employees will be serving the city
council in a staff capacity within the meaning of regulation 18701(a)(2)(B).² They are

² Implicit in the notion of "serves in a staff capacity" is an ongoing relationship between the contractor and
the public agency. Individuals who work on one project or a limited range of projects for the agency are
not considered to be serving an agency in a staff capacity unless those projects extend over a substantial
period of time. (Travis Advice Letter, No. A-96-053; Randolph Advice Letter, No. A-95-045.) Previous

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 6

therefore "public officials" subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions, and we
next consider the possible application of those rules to the circumstances you describe.³

Questions Two and Three: Do the Consultants' Specified Economic Interests
Create a Conflict of Interest in Decisions Involving SPL?

The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official
has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of
the official's economic interests.

The Commission uses an eight-step process in determining whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a particular governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) – (8).) We will consider the circumstances you
describe within this eight-step framework. We have already concluded that the two Blue
Heron employees are "public officials" by reason of their status as "consultants" to the
city council, and that they will be participating in making governmental decisions during
the course of their planned work. We may therefore begin our analysis at step three,
identifying the economic interests of the two consultants.

Step 3. What are the consultants' economic interests?

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising out of
economic interests. These economic interests are specified at section 87103 and are
more fully described at regulations 18703.1 through 18703.5. You ask us to consider
the potential for conflicts of interest arising out of one particular economic interest held
by each consultant – the investment interest in SPL stock owned by one, and the second
consultant's spousal income from SPL.⁴

Commission advice has found that a performance period of more than one year is sufficient to meet this
temporal qualifier, whereas nine months of regular and continuous work is not enough to qualify. (Smith
Advice Letter, No. I-99-316.) The anticipated term of service here, combining stages one and two, is more
than a year, which establishes that the relationship is of sufficient duration that the Blue Heron employees
would be serving the city council "in a staff capacity."
³ Regardless of the presence or absence of a conflict of interest in any particular governmental decision,
because the Blue Heron Employees would be classified as "consultants," they are "public officials" who
are required to file periodic statements of economic interests (Form 700) disclosing any economic interests
or assets which may be affected during the performance of their official duties. (Section 87300 - 87350.)
⁴ In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always has an
economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a 12-month period.
(Regulations 18703.5 and 18705.5.) Your account of the facts does not suggest that decisions in which the
Blue Heron employees might participate would implicate their economic interests in personal finances, but
we mention the possibility for the sake of completeness.

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 7

A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or she is a director,
officer, partner, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a)(d);
regulations 18703.1(a) and (b).) Ownership of stock in SPL valued at approximately
$15,000 gives the consultant who owns it an economic interest in SPL. Should a public
official sell the stock that constitutes an investment interest in a business entity, the
economic interest in the business entity comes to an end once the sale is concluded and
the official no longer has a legal or equitable interest in the stock. (Doane Advice Letter,
No. A-97-211; section 82030(b)(5), copy enclosed.)⁵ Thus the consultant who owns
stock in SPL, assuming that there is no other connection with SPL, has an economic
interest in that business only so long as he or she owns the stock.

A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) The Blue Heron employee whose
spouse was employed on SPL in the Philippines has a community property interest in the
spouse's income. (Section 82030(a).) This consultant will therefore have economic
interest in SPL until such time as his or her community property share of the spouse's
income falls below $500. This will occur when the consultant's spouse has earned or
received less than $1,000 from SPL during the previous twelve months.

Steps 4 and 5. Are the Consultants' Economic Interests Directly or Indirectly Involved
in Any Governmental Decisions, And What Are the Pertinent Materiality Standards?

Our next step is to determine whether the economic interests you have described
will be involved directly or indirectly in any decisions the consultants may participate in
making during the course of their service to the city council. The answer to this question
determines the standard used to determine the level at which an effect of a given decision
on the economic interest will be deemed "material."

Because we do not have full information on specific governmental decisions in
which these consultants may participate, we provide you with the language of regulation
18704.1, which will assist you in making this determination as the details of particular
decisions become known:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,

⁵ Note, however, that the buyer may become an economic interest of the consultant's, as a source of
income, unless the sale is conducted as specified in regulation 82030(b)(12).

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 8

approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.
(b) If a business entity, source of income, or source of a
gift is directly involved in a governmental decision, apply
the materiality standards in California Code of
Regulations, Title 2, section 18705.1(b), section
18705.3(a), or section 18705.4(a), respectively. If a
business entity, source of income, or source of a gift is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, apply the
materiality standards in California Code of Regulations,
Title 2, section 18705.1(c), section 18705.3(b), or section
18705.4(b), respectively."

From what you have told us, we believe that the consultants may be participants
in a number of decisions that would directly involve BearingPoint, while indirectly
involving SPL. But we cannot rule out the possibility that these consultants may
participate in some decisions directly involving SPL. Such determinations can only be
made by applying the law to the facts specific to each decision.

Effects on the economic interests of both consultants would be classified as
"material" under the guidelines provided by regulation 18705.1 (copy enclosed). Under
subdivision (b) of this regulation, effects on business entities that are directly involved in
a given decision are presumed to be material, although this presumption may be rebutted
by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have any financial
effect on the business entity. There is a limited exception in subdivision (b)(2) applicable
when the economic interest derives solely from an investment worth $25,000 or less in
certain large business entities. In such cases, the materiality standard is the same as that
for business entities of comparable size that are only indirectly involved in the decision.

When the business entity is indirectly involved in a governmental decision,
materiality of effect is established in the various subdivisions of regulation 18705.1(c), on
a scale that varies with the size of the business entity. Thus, for example, the materiality
threshold for business entities of relatively modest size is set by regulation 18705.1(c)(4),
which provides that the financial effect on such businesses is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease of the
business's gross revenues, assets or liabilities in the amount of $20,000 or more during
any fiscal year, or if expenses are affected in the amount of $5,000 or more during any
fiscal year. The materiality thresholds are set higher for larger business entities. You can
review the enclosed regulation and determine for yourself the level at which a reasonably
foreseeable effect of a governmental decision becomes material for a business like SPL.

Step 6. Is it Reasonably Foreseeable That a Particular Decision Will Have a Material
Financial Effect on the Consultants' Economic Interests?

After determining the level of economic effect considered to be "material" for a
given economic interest, it is necessary to decide whether it is "reasonably foreseeable"
that the effect of a given decision will reach the level of "material." A financial effect is
considered "reasonably foreseeable" if it is "substantially likely" to occur. (Regulation

File No. A-04-245
Page No. 9

18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether a material financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable at the time a decision is made ultimately depends on facts and
circumstances peculiar to each decision. (In re Thorner, supra.) Because we do not have
all the facts pertinent to such a determination for any decision in which the consultants
may become involved, we cannot offer you more concrete advice at this time.

Steps 7 and 8: Exceptions.

A public official who otherwise would have a conflict of interest in a governmental decision may still participate in making that decision if the circumstances are such
that the "public generally" rule might be invoked. This rule applies when the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a decision upon a public official's economic interests is
"material," but not distinguishable from foreseeable effects on a significant segment of
the public generally. (Section 87103; regulation 18707(a).) Your account of the facts
does not suggest that this exception would be applicable to the consultants you describe.

Finally, an official with a conflict of interest in a decision may still participate in
that decision if the "legally required participation" rule is applicable. (Section 87101;
regulation 18708.) This is an exception that typically applies when an agency is unable
to assemble a quorum of its members without the participation of an official who has a
conflict of interest. Again, it seems unlikely that this rule will find application under the
circumstances you have described.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

Under the terms of this rqgulation, a business emtity cannot be a "consultant "
since the term is applied only to an "individual," that is, to a natrral persron. Thus only
nahral persons actually performing duties under the contract may be "consultants."
(Sanchu Advice Letter, No. I-03-173-) Employees of abusiness who do not perform
work under the contract, or whose duties do not meet the definition of "consultant;" are
not "consultants" under the Act. (Del Guercio Advice Letter, No. I-01-l16.) Our first
order of business, therefore, is to identifu the individuals performing duties under the
contract, and then to determine whether those duties are such that the responsible
individuals meet the definition of "consultant." In your inquiry you identifr two such
persons, and we will confine our discussion to those two Blue Heron ernployees.
Regulation 18701(a)(2) establishes two standards for qualification as a consultant.
An individual who satisfies either standard is a "consultant" as defined by the Act. First,
an individual may be a "consultant" if he or she perfonns, ptrrsuant to contract, any of the
actions described in suMivisions (a)(2)(AXl)-(7) ofregulation 18701. Alternatively, a,
individual maybe a consultant if he or she 'Seryes in staffcapacity with the agenct''
under subdivision (aX2XB).
As we understand the facts, neither of the two identified Blue Heron employees is
authorized to make final, binding decisions on behalf ofthe city, and in that case neither
would qualifu as a "@nsultanf'under subdivisions (a[2)(A)(1)-(7) of regulation l87}l.
It appears that both of them, however, would be authorized and expected under two
successive "consultancy'' contracts to provide ongoing technical recommendations and
advice to the city on the developmen! managernen! and execution of the BearingPoint
agreement through stages involving purchase, installation and integration of the CUSP
system. You indicate that the stage I contract will last approximately seven months, and
that the stage 2 contract will have a term of twelve months.
From the limited information available to us, we conclude that both of these Blue
Heron employees would be "consultants" to the city council. First, it appears from your
account of the facts they will at least occasionally be called on to "participate in making a
govemmental decision" regarding the city's dealings with BearingPoint. Regulation
18702.2(b) specifies that a person "participates in making a govemmental decision" when
he or she:
"(b) Advises or makes recornmendations to the
decisionmaker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by:
(l) Conducting resemch or making any investigation
which requires the exercise ofjudgment on thepart of the
official and the purpose of which is to influence a
governmental decision referemced in Title 2, Califomia
Code of Regulations, section I 8701 (a)(2)(A); or
(2) Preparing or presenting any repor! analysis, or opiniorq
orally, or in writing which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and thepurpose of
which is to influenoe a govemmental d*ision referenced in
T itle 2, Califomia Code of Regulations, section
t87at@)QXA),"
It also appears that both of these Blue Heron ernployees will be serving the city
council in a staff capacity within the meaning of regulation 18701(a)(2XB).' They are
'Implicit in the notion of "serves in a staff capaci!y''is anongoingrelationship between the contractor and
the public agency. Individuals who work on one project or a limited range ofprojects for the agency are
not considered to be serving an agency in a staffcapacity unless those projects extend over a substantial
period of time. (Travis Advice Letter, No. A-96-053; Randolph Advice Letter, No- A-95-045.) Previous


FileNo. A-04-245
therefore "public officials" subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions, and we
next consider the possible application of those rules to the circumstances you describe.3
Questions Two and Three: Do the Consultants'Specilied Economic fnterests
Create a Conflict of fnterest in Decisions Involving SpL?
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making or using his or her official position in any way to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official
has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable ftom its effect on the
public generally on the official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of
the official's economic interests.
The Commission uses an eight-step process in determining whether a public
official has a disqualifuing conflict of interest in aparticulargovernmental decision.
(Regulation 18700, subdivisions (bxl) - (S).) We will considerthe circumstances you
describe within this eight-step framework- We have already ooncluded that the two Blue
Heron employees are "public officials" by reason of their status as "consultants" to the
city council, and that they will beparticipating in making governmental decisions during
the course of their planned work. We may therefore begin our analysis at step three,
identiffing the economic interests of the two consultants.
Step 3. What are the consultants, economic interests?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising out of
economic interests. These economic interests are specified at section 87103 and are
more fully described at regulations l8713.l through 18703-5- You ask us to consider
the potential for conflicts of interest arising out of one particular economic interest held
by each consultant - the investnent interest in SPL stock owned by one, and the second
consultant's spousal income &om SPL.a
Commission advice has ford that a performance period of more than one year is sufficient to meet this
temporal qualifier, whry1s nine months ofregular and continuous work is not enough to qualiry- $mith
Advice Irtt€r, No. I-99-316.) Th anticipated term of service here, combining stages oncand two, is more
than a ysan', which establishes tbat the relatiorship is of suffici€,nt duration ftat-Oe Btr fferm eryloyees
would be serving the city council 'in a staffcapacifr.-
' Regardless of the presence or abserce of a conflict of interest in any particulac governmtal decision,
because tbe Blue Heron Eryloyees uould be classified as 'consultab,- 6ey re bublic officials- nfio
are required to file periodic statements of economic intorests (Form 700) disclosing-any economic interests
:: *":9 which may be affected during the performance of their official duties- (Sections S72W-S7350.)
" In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always has an
economic interest in his or herpersonal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, asse* oi liabilities ofthe
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-monthperid.
(Regulations 18703.5 and 18705.5.) Your account of the facts does not suggest that decisions in which the
Blue Heron employees might participate would implicate their economic interests in personal finances, but
we mention the possibility for the sake of completeness.
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or she is a director,
offi cer, partner, employeg or holds any position of management. (Section 37 I 03 (a)(d);
regulations 18703.1(a) and (b).) Ownership of stock in SPL valued at approximately
$ 15,000 gves the consultant who owns it an economic interest in SPL. Should u p.rbli"
official sell the stock that constitutes an investnent interest in a business entity, the
economic interest in the business entity comes to an end once the sale is concluded and
the official no longer has a legal or equitable interest in the stock- (Doane Advice Letter,
No. A-97-21l; section 82030(bX5), copy enclosed.)s Thus the consultant who owns
stock in SPL, assuming that there is no other oonnection with SP! has an economic
interest in that business only so long as he or she owns the stock.
A public official has an economic interest in any source of incomg including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 13703.3.) The Blue Heron employee whose
spouse was employed by SPL in the Philippines has a community property interest in the
spouse's income. (Section 82030(a).) This conzultant vrill therefore have an economic
interest in SPL until such time as his or her community property share of the qlouse,s
income falls below $500. This will occurwhen the consultant's qro.use has earned or
received less than $1,000 from sPL during theprevious twelve months.
Steps 4 and 5. Are the Consultants'Economic ffieres9 Directly or fndirecfly fnvolved
in Any Gavernmental Decisions, And |Yha Are the Pertinent Materiatity Standards?
Ournext step is to determine whetherthe economic interests you have described
will be involved directly or indirectly in any decisions the consultants may participate in
making during the course of their service to the city council. The answer to this question
determines the standard used to determinethe level at which an effect of a given decision
on the economic interest will be deemed "material--
Because we do not have full infonnation on specific governmental decisions in
which these consultants may participate, we provide you with the language of rqgulation
l87M,.l, which will assist you inmaking this determination as the detailsofparticular
decisions become known:
"(a) A p€rson, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directlyinvolved in a
decision before an official's agency when ^\at p€rso&
either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceoding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, clairl appeal, or similm
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject o{, the
proceeding conceming the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
t Note, howevet, that the buyer maybecome an economic interest of the consultant's, as a source of
income, unless the sale is conducted as specified in regulation S2030(bXl2).


FileNo. A-04-245
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.
(b) If a business entity, source of income, or source of a
gift is directly involved in a governmental decision, apply
the materiality standards in Califomia Code of
Regulations, Title 2, section t 3705.1(b), section
18705.3(a), or section 18705.a@), respectively. lf a
business entity, source of income, or source of a gift is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, apply the
materiality standards in Califomia Code of Regulations,
Title2, section 18705.1(c), section 1S705.3O), or section
1 8705.4(b), respectively. "
From what you have told us, we believe that the consultants may be participants
in a number of decisions that would directly involve BearingPoint, while indirectly
involving SPL. But we cannot rule out the possibility that these consultants may
participate in some decisions directly involving SPL. Such determinations can only be
made by applying the law to the facts specific to each decision.
Effects on the economic interests ofboth consultants would be classified as
"material" under the guidelines provided by regulation 18705.1 (copy enclosed). Under
subdivision (b) of this regulation, effects on business entities that are directly involved in
a given decision are presumed to be material, although this presumption may be rebutted
by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have any financial
effect on the business entity. There is a limited exception in suMivision (b)(2) applicable
when the economic interest derives solely from an inveshnent worth $25,000 or less in
certain large business entities. In such cases, the materiality standard is the same as that
for business entities of comparable size that are only indirectly involved in the decision.
When the business entity is indirectly involved in a governmental decision,
materiality of effect is established in the various subdivisions ofregulation 18705.1(c), on
a scale that vmies with the size of the business entity. Thus, for example, the materiality
threshold for business entities of relatively modest size is set by regulation I S705.1(c)(4),
which provides that the financial effect on such businesses is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease of the
business's gross revenues, assets or liabilities in the amount of $20,000 or more during
any fiscal year, or if expenses are effected in the amount of $5,000 ormore during any
fiscal year. The materiality thresholds are set higher for largerbusiness entities. you can
review ttre enclosd regulation and determine for yourself the level at which a reasonably
foreseeable effect of a governmental decision becomes material for a business like SpL.
Step 6. fs it Reasonably Foreseeable That a Particular Decision Will Have a Material
Financial Elfect on the Consultants'Economic fnturests?
After determining the level of economic effect considered to be "material" for a
$lven economic interest, it is necessary to decide whether it is "reasonably foreseeable"
that the effect of a given decision will reach the level of "material." A financial effect is
considered "reasonably foreseeable" if it is "substantially likely''to occur. (Regulation
18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether amoterial financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable at the time a decision is made ultimately depends on facts and
circumstances peculiar to each decision. (In re Thorner, supro.) Because we do not have
all the facts pertinent to such a determination for any decision in which the consultants
may become involved, we cannot offer you more concrete advice at this time.
Steps 7 and 8: Exceptions
A public official who otherwise would have a conflict of interest in a govern-
mental decision may still participate in making that decision if the circumstances are such
that the "public generally''rule might be invoked- This rule applies when the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a decision qpon apublic official's economic interests is
"material," but not distinguishable from foreseeable effets on a significant segment of
thepublic generally. (Section 87103; regulation lS7O7(a).) Your-account of the facts
does not suggest that this exception would be applicable to the conzultants you describe.
Finally, an official with a conflict of interest in a decision may still participate in
that decision if the "legally required participation" rule is applicable. (Section 87101;
regulation 18708.) This is an exception that typically applies when an agency is unable
to assemble a quorum of its members without the participation of an official who has a
conflict of interest- Again, it seems unlikely that this rule will find application under the
circumstances you have described.

"consultant" under the Act, and obligated as such to file a Form 700?
2- \f the answer to the first question is'Jres," would ownership of SPL WorldGroup
("SPL') stock create a conflict of interest under the Act in decisions involving SPL and,
if so, would the conflict of interest be removed by sale of the stock?
3. If the answer to the first question is 'lres," would the income earned by the spouse of
one of these "consultants" c:reate a conflict of interest under the Act in decisions
involving SPL?

CONCLUSION: Question l: Yes, both Blue Heron employees would meet the Act,s definition of
"consultant" under the facts presented, and would therefore be "public officials,, subject
to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.
Questions 2 and 3: As long as the consultants retain economic interests in SpL, they may
have conflicts of interest in particular governmental decisions, although without more
specific information on upcoming decisions, ure can only offer general guidance on
identiffing potential conflicts of interest. The consultant whose eoonomic interest in SpL
is grounded in stock ownership would no longerhave an economic interest in SpL once
he or she sold that stock. An economic interest in SPL based on income to a oonsultant,s
spouse will come to an end with thepassage oftime- Specifically, the consultant would
no longer have an economic interest in SPL as a source of income once the consultant,s
communityproperty share of that income falls below $500, which would occur when the
consultant's spouse has received or earned less than $I,000 in inoome from SpL during
the precedi ng I 2 months.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 January 20, 2005 Karin Murabito, Deputy City Attorney City of San Jose Office of the City Attorney 151 West Mission Street San Jose, CA 95110 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-04-245 Dear Ms. Murabito: This letter is in response to your request, on behalf of the City of San Jose and the City's Finance Director Scott Johnson, for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Would either of the Blue Heron employees you describe be classified as a "consultant" under the Act, and obligated as such to file a Form 700? 2. If the answer to the first question is "yes," would ownership of SPL WorldGroup ("SPL") stock create a conflict of interest under the Act in decisions involving SPL and, if so, would the conflict of interest be removed by sale of the stock? 3. If the answer to the first question is "yes," would the income earned by the spouse of one of these "consultants" create a conflict of interest under the Act in decisions involving SPL? ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-04-245 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS Question 1: Yes, both Blue Heron employees would meet the Act's definition of "consultant" under the facts presented, and would therefore be "public officials" subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions. Questions 2 and 3: As long as the consultants retain economic interests in SPL, they may have conflicts of interest in particular governmental decisions, although without more specific information on upcoming decisions, we can only offer general guidance on identifying potential conflicts of interest. The consultant whose economic interest in SPL is grounded in stock ownership would no longer have an economic interest in SPL once he or she sold that stock. An economic interest in SPL based on income to a consultant's spouse will come to an end with the passage of time. Specifically, the consultant would no longer have an economic interest in SPL as a source of income once the consultant's community property share of that income falls below $500, which would occur when the consultant's spouse has received or earned less than $1,000 in income from SPL during the preceding 12 months. FACTS In June 2004, the City of San Jose selected BearingPoint/People Soft as its vendor for the planned purchase, installation, and integration of a computerized utility billing system, referred to as the CUSP or the CUSP Project. The CUSP Project is divided into two stages. During stage 1, which may run for seven months, BearingPoint will provide the city with a detailed analysis of how the city's existing system will interface with the new PeopleSoft system. During stage 2, an implementation phase expected to take twelve months, BearingPoint will install the PeopleSoft system. City staff will
